statement.markdown:
Write a function to validate a Git tag name. A valid tag name should only contain letters (both uppercase and lowercase), digits, and special characters that are commonly used in Git tags. A valid tag name should start with a letter (both uppercase and lowercase) or digit and cannot be empty. For this problem, we assume that a valid tag name should not be longer than 40 characters.

You can assume that the input is a non-empty string and all characters in the string are either letters (both uppercase and lowercase), digits, or special characters that are commonly used in Git tags.

Your function should return `true` if the tag name is valid and `false` otherwise.
statement.inputFormat: A single string representing the Git tag name.
statement.outputFormat: A boolean indicating whether the tag name is valid.
statement.notes: Note that this problem is a simplification of the actual Git tag name validation rules.

constraints.description: The length of the input string

examples.explanation: This is a valid Git tag name.
This is also a valid Git tag name.
This is not a valid Git tag name because it contains a hyphen.
This is not a valid Git tag name because it is too short.
This is not a valid Git tag name because it is too long.

hints.text: Use a regular expression to match the input string.

editorial.title: Fixing Git Tag Name Validation
editorial.intuition: The key idea is to use a regular expression to match the input string. The regular expression should match any string that starts with a letter or digit and only contains letters, digits, and special characters that are commonly used in Git tags.
editorial.bruteForce: One possible brute-force approach is to iterate over each character in the input string and check whether it is a valid character. However, this approach is not efficient and may not work for large input strings.
editorial.betterSolution: A better solution is to use a regular expression to match the input string. The regular expression should match any string that starts with a letter or digit and only contains letters, digits, and special characters that are commonly used in Git tags.
editorial.optimalSolution: The optimal solution is to use the following regular expression to match the input string: `/^[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9-._*]+$/.test(s)`. This regular expression checks whether the input string starts with a letter or digit and only contains letters, digits, and special characters that are commonly used in Git tags.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: The correctness of the solution can be proved by considering all possible cases of input strings.
editorial.dryRun:
Here is a dry run of the solution for each example in the problem statement: 

Example 1: `console.log(/^[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9-._*]+$/.test('v1.0')); // true`

Example 2: `console.log(/^[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9-._*]+$/.test('v1.0.1')); // true`

Example 3: `console.log(/^[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9-._*]+$/.test('tag-v1.0')); // false`

Example 4: `console.log(/^[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9-._*]+$/.test('v')); // false`

Example 5: `console.log(/^[a-zA-Z][a-zA-Z0-9-._*]+$/.test('v1.0.1.2')); // false`
commonMistakes: Not using a regular expression to match the input string
Not checking whether the input string starts with a letter or digit
